locked_facts: Indian mains: V_rms = 220V, f = 50Hz, therefore V_m ≈ 311V
Average of AC over a complete cycle = 0
Average over a half cycle = 2V_m / π ≈ 0.637 × V_m
RMS is defined to produce same heating effect as equivalent DC
truth_statements: V_rms = V_m divided by root 2, NOT V_m divided by 2
Indian 220V supply has peak voltage of approximately 311V
Complete cycle average of AC is exactly zero
RMS voltage is always less than peak voltage for sinusoidal AC

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show horizontal dashed lines at both V_m = 311V and V_rms = 220V on the same waveform. Student sees the plug in their wall carries 311V peaks — not 220V constantly. The 220V label on their charger is the RMS equivalent, not the instantaneous value.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two horizontal reference lines — orange at V_m, blue at V_rms — with the sine wave passing through both, clearly above V_rms half the time and below it half the time

simulation_states.label: Sinusoidal waveform — v(t) = V_m sin(ωt)
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Clean sine wave labelled with equation. x-axis = time, y-axis = voltage. Wave oscillates symmetrically above and below zero.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The wave is perfectly symmetric — equally positive and negative. This is why the simple average is zero.

simulation_states.label: Peak values marked
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Dashed horizontal lines at +V_m and -V_m with numerical values. Time period T marked along x-axis. One full cycle highlighted.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: V_m is the maximum instantaneous value — the wave only reaches this at one instant per half cycle

simulation_states.label: RMS line appears — the aha moment
simulation_states.what_student_sees: A blue dashed line appears at V_rms = V_m/√2. For the default V_m = 311V, this sits at 220V. The sine wave crosses this line twice per cycle.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: V_rms is NOT halfway between 0 and V_m. It is V_m/√2 ≈ 0.707 × V_m. This is the effective value — what a DC source of this voltage would produce in terms of heating.

simulation_states.label: DC equivalence demonstrated
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Split screen: AC at 311V peak on the left, DC at 220V on the right, both connected to identical resistors. Heat produced (represented by glow intensity) is identical.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: This is the fundamental meaning of RMS — same power delivered as DC of that value. That is why we rate appliances at 220V even though the actual peak is 311V.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: V_rms is the average of V_m and 0, which is V_m/2
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: V_rms = V_m/√2 ≈ 0.707 × V_m. The RMS is computed by squaring the function, averaging it, then taking the square root — a fundamentally different operation from arithmetic average.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show both values numerically on the graph: for V_m = 100V, draw a line at V_m/2 = 50V labeled WRONG and another at V_rms = 70.7V labeled CORRECT. Student must see they are different and understand why.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: The 220V from the mains is always 220V — a constant voltage like a battery
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Mains 220V is an RMS value. The instantaneous voltage oscillates sinusoidally between +311V and -311V fifty times per second.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the full sine wave of Indian mains supply with V_m = 311V marked. Show the 220V RMS line. Animate the wave completing one cycle in 1/50 second = 20ms.
